name: mysql_gap_lock_phantom
description: MySQL InnoDB gap-lock behavior under REPEATABLE READ. InnoDB uses next-key locking on index ranges to prevent phantoms, but the locked range depends on which index the optimizer selects. This example shows how the same predicate can exhibit different locking behavior with different indexes.
references: MySQL 8.0 Reference Manual, Section 15.7.1: InnoDB Locking
MySQL 8.0 Reference Manual, Section 15.7.3: Locks Set by Different SQL Statements
Berenson et al. A Critique of ANSI SQL Isolation Levels. SIGMOD 1995
setup_sql: CREATE TABLE accounts (id INT PRIMARY KEY, balance INT NOT NULL, region VARCHAR(32) NOT NULL, INDEX idx_balance(balance), INDEX idx_region(region))

transactions.label: Attempt insert into gap locked by T1

schedule: T1: SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE balance BETWEEN 150 AND 350
-- InnoDB acquires next-key locks on idx_balance gap (100, 200], (200, 300], (300, 500]
-- BLOCKS: gap lock on idx_balance covers (200, 300) which includes balance=250

expected_outcome.mechanism: InnoDB next-key gap locking on idx_balance B-tree
expected_outcome.note: If the optimizer chose idx_region instead, the gap locks would cover different ranges and the insert might NOT be blocked — this is the index-dependent behavior that IsoSpec models

gaps_locked: gap before 'EU' records in region index
scenario_b.insert_blocked: depends on region value, not balance range
